narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 56% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your Year 1 school decile of 2—well below the typical student's 5.7—your even lower Year 3 decile of 1 compared to the average 5.5, an LSAT score of 30 versus the usual 36.7, an undergraduate GPA of 2.4 against the standard 3.2, low family income, and your background as a Black female planning full-time studies. When the model looked at students like you, it saw patterns where these traits often link to bar exam struggles, unlike the majority who are White, have higher deciles, and stronger test scores. The strongest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile, as it signals recent academic challenges that stand out sharply. Next in influence came your Year 1 decile, reinforcing a trend of below-average performance early on. Your LSAT score ranked third in impact, being notably lower than what succeeds for most applicants. Family income followed closely, with low brackets historically correlating with tougher odds in this dataset. Your racial background as Black, underrepresented at just 4.6% compared to 83.8% White students, also nudged the prediction downward, as did your undergraduate GPA. Being female and full-time, while common (43.6% female, 92.7% full-time), had the least sway but still tilted slightly toward the riskier side. Overall, no single factor outweighed the others dramatically, but together they painted a riskier picture than for typical admits. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 36, build stronger academic habits through targeted prep courses, and seek bar exam tutoring early, as past grades like deciles and GPA are fixed but preparation skills aren't. With dedication, students in similar spots have turned predictions around by prioritizing test prep and support networks. We encourage reapplying after showing these improvements. Keep pushing—you have the drive to beat these odds.
